chunk_summary: This section highlights red flags for endocrine, genetic, and hypothalamic causes of obesity in children, emphasizing targeted evaluation to avoid mismanagement and ensure specialist-led care when secondary causes are suspected.
learning_objectives: Identify clinical patterns that suggest endocrine, genetic, or hypothalamic obesity in pediatric patients.
Understand the importance of distinguishing primary obesity from secondary causes to guide appropriate management.
Recognize medication-associated obesity and coordinate care to mitigate metabolic risks.
key_facts: Most pediatric obesity is primary (exogenous), but secondary causes should be suspected in cases with poor linear growth, delayed puberty, or cushingoid features.
Severe obesity beginning before age 5, accompanied by hyperphagia, developmental delay, or dysmorphism, may indicate genetic or syndromic obesity.
Medication-associated obesity is linked to atypical antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and chronic glucocorticoids.
Hypothalamic obesity may present with rapid-onset severe obesity after CNS insult, persistent headache, or visual symptoms.
Explicitly naming hyperphagia as biology-driven reduces stigma and improves family engagement.
reference_quote: Clinical patterns that should trigger targeted endocrine or neurologic evaluation include poor linear growth, delayed puberty, and cushingoid features. (Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline, 2017)
